refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can continuously or periodically capture energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, telemetry, or maintenance reporting system. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated instrumentation over a representative duty cycle.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and report the ratio of energy consumed to work output.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors in the equipment; compute energy_per_ton continuously onboard; stream results to central reporting system.

DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel/power draw logs (existing operational records) and cross-reference with independent work-output measurement (scale, GPS, or task logs); reconcile periodically to derive energy_per_ton.
DP4: Deploy dual-path measurement: primary path uses integrated sensors (energy + work); secondary path uses operational logs for validation; report consensus metric when agreement threshold is met.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and work-output measurement are logically decoupled: energy is measured at fuel/power supply boundary; work is measured at delivery point (independent sensor or operational record). Decoupling reduces risk of single-point failure affecting both numerator and denominator.

Offline computation via aggregation pipeline introduces temporal decoupling: measurements can be collected asynchronously and reconciled later, reducing real-time coupling between field equipment and reporting system.

Risk: If energy source and work-output measurement share a common calibration standard or environmental dependency (e.g., temperature), coupling may emerge. Mitigation: use independent calibration chains and environmental compensation.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low functional coupling because energy input and work output are measured at distinct physical/logical boundaries. The aggregation pipeline is a passive data-fusion layer, not a control loop, further reducing coupling. DP2 would exhibit higher coupling (onboard computation ties energy and work measurement tightly to a single device). DP3 introduces coupling through reliance on operational logs that may not be synchronized with actual energy consumption. DP4 adds complexity without reducing coupling.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel tank, power meter) is accessible and instrumentable without disrupting operations. Risk: If energy source is remote or shared across multiple work units, attribution of energy to specific work output becomes ambiguous. Mitigation: establish clear energy allocation rules (e.g., per-shift, per-equipment-ID).

Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured independently and reliably at point of delivery. Risk: If delivery point is not well-defined (e.g., material spread over area) or measurement is manual, accuracy and repeatability suffer. Mitigation: standardize delivery point definition and use automated measurement (scale, volumetric sensor).

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average out transient inefficiencies and measurement noise. Risk: Short evaluation windows may yield high variance in energy_per_ton, making it difficult to assess whether E_target is met. Mitigation: define minimum evaluation period (e.g., 1 week, 1 month) in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Offline aggregation pipeline has sufficient data integrity and traceability. Risk: Data loss, clock skew, or missing records during aggregation could invalidate energy_per_ton calculation. Mitigation: implement data validation, checksums, and audit logs in aggregation pipeline.

Assumption: Environmental and operational conditions (load, terrain, weather) are representative of intended use. Risk: If evaluation is conducted under atypical conditions, measured energy_per_ton may not reflect real-world performance. Mitigation: document conditions during evaluation and establish confidence intervals.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output, computed as energy_per_ton = (total energy input measured at source over evaluation period) / (total tonnage moved measured at delivery point over evaluation period), such that energy_per_ton ≤ E_target. Measurement shall be decoupled at source and delivery boundaries, with offline aggregation and validation to ensure data integrity and traceability. Evaluation period shall be ≥ [specify: 1 week | 1 month | other] to ensure statistical significance. Environmental and operational conditions during evaluation shall be documented and representative of intended worksite use.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level data aggregation, energy accounting, and efficiency computation pipeline that collects instrumented energy input and work output telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, performs offline ratio analysis, and reports aggregate efficiency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives instrumented energy consumption (fuel/electricity) and work output (tonnage moved) measurements from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules at regular intervals.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Optionally sends efficiency-based task recommendations or operational guidance back to Teleoperation based on computed energy-to-work ratios.

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level aggregator for cross-subsystem telemetry collection, offline data processing, and efficiency computation. Energy and work instrumentation data flows naturally into this layer via Telemetry interface. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must instrument their sensors (owned locally), but aggregation logic remains centralized.

Weak containment: Vehicle owns fuel/electricity instrumentation at source, but work output (tonnage moved) is a delivery-point metric that depends on load, terrain, and Teleoperation control. Ratio computation requires cross-subsystem data; placing it in Vehicle creates tight coupling to Teleoperation and forces Vehicle to own system-level analytics logic.

Weak containment: Teleoperation controls work output but does not own energy input instrumentation (Vehicle responsibility). Placing ratio computation here creates asymmetric ownership and forces Teleoperation to depend on Vehicle telemetry for energy data. System-level efficiency analysis is not a Teleoperation concern.

Very weak containment: Safety Services is orthogonal to energy efficiency accounting. Placing energy-to-work ratio computation here violates separation of concerns and creates spurious coupling between safety and operational efficiency domains.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (instrument energy input at source, instrument work output at delivery point, compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) it is a system-level cross-subsystem concern that requires aggregating data from both Vehicle (energy) and Teleoperation (work output); (2) offline computation is a natural fit for a solution-layer analytics pipeline; (3) Vehicle and Teleoperation retain ownership of their respective instrumentation (low diffusion), while NGHE Solution owns the aggregation and ratio logic (high cohesion); (4) the Telemetry interface provides a clean, pre-defined channel for data ingestion.
containment_risks: Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose instrumented energy and work output telemetry via Telemetry interface; if either subsystem lacks native instrumentation, DP realization is blocked.

Offline computation implies latency between data collection and ratio availability; if real-time efficiency feedback is later required, DP may need revision to move computation into Vehicle or Teleoperation.
Data aggregation pipeline must handle missing, delayed, or malformed telemetry; robustness of offline computation depends on data quality controls upstream.

If efficiency ratios are used to drive closed-loop control (e.g., adaptive fuel injection), the offline nature of DP may be insufficient; DP may need to be split into offline aggregation (NGHE Solution) and online feedback (Vehicle).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete measurement and reporting strategy: (1) instrument energy input at source (fuel/electricity), (2) instrument work output at delivery point (tonnage moved), (3) compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline. The DP is solution-agnostic and logically sound. It maps cleanly to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle owns energy instrumentation but does not own work output measurement; work output (tonnage moved) is a delivery-point metric that depends on Teleoperation control and load state. If Vehicle attempts to compute or validate energy-to-work ratios locally, it creates tight coupling and diffusion of system-level analytics logic.

Teleoperation controls work output but does not own energy input data; if Teleoperation is tasked with real-time efficiency feedback or adaptive control based on energy-to-work ratios, it must depend on Vehicle telemetry, creating asymmetric ownership and potential latency issues.

Offline computation in NGHE Solution introduces latency between data collection and ratio availability. If operational policies or closed-loop control later require real-time efficiency feedback (e.g., adaptive fuel injection or load balancing), the offline DP may be insufficient and require splitting into online (Vehicle/Teleoperation) and offline (NGHE Solution) components.

Data quality and completeness: NGHE Solution's aggregation pipeline depends on Vehicle and Teleoperation exposing instrumented telemetry via the Telemetry interface. If either subsystem lacks native instrumentation or produces malformed/delayed data, DP realization is blocked or degraded. No upstream data validation or fallback strategy is specified.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for DP1. Containment score of 0.85 is justified: (1) system-level cross-subsystem concern naturally owned at solution layer; (2) offline aggregation and ratio computation are cohesive within a data pipeline; (3) Vehicle and Teleoperation retain ownership of instrumentation (low diffusion at subsystem level); (4) Telemetry interface provides clean, pre-defined data ingestion channel. Vehicle (0.4 containment) and Teleoperation (0.35 containment) are correctly identified as weak candidates due to asymmetric data ownership and risk of coupling system-level analytics to subsystem concerns. Safety Services (0.1 containment) is correctly excluded as orthogonal to operational efficiency.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from instrumented data (energy_used, ton_moved) aggregated by NGHE Solution. Verification requires: (1) confirmation that Vehicle and Teleoperation expose energy and work output telemetry via Telemetry interface; (2) validation of data quality and completeness over the agreed evaluation period; (3) offline computation of energy_per_ton ratio and comparison to E_target. No real-time or closed-loop verification is required at this gate.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the aggregation pipeline, offline ratio computation, and reporting. Vehicle owns energy instrumentation at source. Teleoperation owns work output instrumentation at delivery point. Interfaces are defined (Telemetry in, Tasking out). However, allocation clarity is contingent on: (1) explicit specification of telemetry schema (energy units, work output units, sampling frequency, data format); (2) definition of 'agreed evaluation period' and data completeness thresholds; (3) clarification of whether Tasking interface (efficiency-based recommendations) is in scope for this FR or deferred to a separate FR.

recommended_revisions: Specify telemetry schema: Define units (e.g., kWh or liters for energy, metric tons for work output), sampling frequency, timestamp precision, and data format (JSON, Avro, etc.) for energy and work output measurements flowing via Telemetry interface. This removes ambiguity in DP realization.

Define data quality acceptance criteria: Specify minimum data completeness (e.g., ≥95% of expected telemetry samples received), maximum latency (e.g., ≤5 minutes), and handling of missing/malformed data (e.g., interpolation, exclusion, or failure mode). This mitigates the risk of degraded DP realization due to upstream data quality issues.

Clarify evaluation period and baseline: Define the 'agreed evaluation period' (e.g., 30 days, 100 operating hours) and whether E_target is a single aggregate value or a time-series threshold. Specify whether baseline energy_per_ton is measured before or after NGHE Solution deployment.

Defer or scope Tasking interface: The Tasking interface (efficiency-based recommendations back to Teleoperation) is mentioned but not required by the FR. Clarify whether this is in scope for the current FR or deferred to a separate FR (e.g., 'The NGHE solution shall enable adaptive operational guidance based on efficiency metrics'). If deferred, remove Tasking from the IM boundary to reduce scope creep.

Address offline computation latency: Document the acceptable latency between data collection and ratio availability. If operational policies require real-time efficiency feedback (e.g., for load balancing or fuel optimization), consider splitting DP1 into: (1) offline aggregation and reporting (NGHE Solution), (2) online efficiency feedback (Vehicle or Teleoperation). This prevents future DP revision.

Specify closed-loop control boundary: If efficiency ratios are later used to drive closed-loop control (e.g., adaptive fuel injection), clarify whether this is a separate FR or an extension of the current FR. If separate, ensure the DP boundary does not assume real-time computation in NGHE Solution.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically, but the FR-IM gate identifies critical allocation clarity gaps and the DP-IM gate surfaces significant diffusion risks that must be resolved before decomposition proceeds. The recommended revisions are not cosmetic; they address: (1) ambiguous telemetry schema that blocks DP realization verification, (2) undefined data quality thresholds that create latency and failure mode risks, (3) scope creep via the Tasking interface (efficiency-based recommendations), and (4) unresolved offline computation latency that may conflict with future closed-loop control requirements. Given high safety criticality, these gaps must be closed at the current level before sub-FRs are derived. Proceeding to decomposition without these clarifications risks propagating ambiguity and diffusion into lower levels.

revise.why: The FR statement 'The NGHE solution shall measure and report energy-to-work efficiency (energy_per_ton) to enable operational optimization' is logically sound but under-specifies critical constraints and scope boundaries. Revisions required: (1) Add explicit acceptance criteria for telemetry schema (units, sampling frequency, data format). (2) Add data quality acceptance criteria (minimum completeness, maximum latency, malformed data handling). (3) Clarify evaluation period and baseline definition. (4) Explicitly exclude or defer the Tasking interface (efficiency-based recommendations) to prevent scope creep. (5) Add a constraint on computation latency: specify whether real-time or offline computation is acceptable, and document the acceptable delay between data collection and ratio availability. (6) If closed-loop control (adaptive fuel injection, load balancing) is anticipated, add a note deferring this to a separate FR to prevent DP revision. Revised FR should read: 'The NGHE solution shall measure and report energy-to-work efficiency (energy_per_ton) via offline aggregation of instrumented telemetry (energy_used in kWh, ton_moved in metric tons) collected over an agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days), with data completeness ≥95% and computation latency ≤[X hours], enabling post-hoc operational analysis. Real-time efficiency feedback and closed-loop control are out of scope and deferred to separate FRs.'

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems will expose energy and work output telemetry via a pre-defined Telemetry interface with consistent schema, units, and sampling frequency.
Data quality and completeness are sufficient (≥95% of expected samples) to compute meaningful energy_per_ton ratios without interpolation or exclusion.
Offline computation latency (hours to days) is acceptable for the intended use case (post-hoc operational analysis, not real-time control).
E_target is a single aggregate threshold (e.g., energy_per_ton ≤ 0.5 kWh/ton) rather than a time-series or adaptive threshold.
The Tasking interface (efficiency-based recommendations back to Teleoperation) is not required by this FR and can be deferred or scoped separately.
Safety criticality applies to the accuracy and completeness of efficiency reporting, not to real-time closed-loop control (which is out of scope).
open_risks: Telemetry schema ambiguity: Vehicle and Teleoperation may use different units, sampling frequencies, or data formats for energy and work output, causing DP realization to fail or require ad-hoc translation logic in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Specify telemetry schema in revised FR and validate via interface contract.

Data quality degradation: If Vehicle or Teleoperation produce incomplete, delayed, or malformed telemetry, NGHE Solution's aggregation pipeline may compute invalid or misleading efficiency ratios. Mitigation: Define data quality acceptance criteria and implement upstream validation/fallback logic.

Offline computation latency conflict: If operational policies later require real-time efficiency feedback (e.g., for adaptive fuel injection or load balancing), the offline DP will be insufficient and require splitting into online and offline components. Mitigation: Clarify acceptable latency in revised FR and defer real-time control to separate FR.

Scope creep via Tasking interface: The DP mentions Tasking (efficiency-based recommendations), but this is not required by the FR. If Tasking is included in IM scope, it creates additional ownership and interface complexity. Mitigation: Explicitly exclude Tasking from current FR scope or define it as a separate FR.

Evaluation period and baseline ambiguity: The FR does not specify the 'agreed evaluation period' or whether E_target is measured before or after NGHE Solution deployment. This creates ambiguity in acceptance criteria. Mitigation: Define evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 100 operating hours) and baseline measurement protocol in revised FR.

Safety criticality mismatch: The FR is marked as high safety criticality, but the DP (offline aggregation and reporting) does not inherently address safety concerns. Clarify whether safety criticality applies to efficiency reporting accuracy or to downstream closed-loop control (which is out of scope). Mitigation: Revise FR to explicitly scope safety criticality to data accuracy and completeness, not to real-time control.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: Document the schema, units, sampling frequency, timestamp precision, and data format for energy and work output measurements. Validate that Vehicle and Teleoperation can expose this data via the Telemetry interface.

Data quality acceptance criteria: Define minimum data completeness (e.g., ≥95% of expected samples), maximum latency (e.g., ≤5 minutes), and handling of missing/malformed data (e.g., interpolation, exclusion, or failure mode).

Evaluation period and baseline protocol: Specify the 'agreed evaluation period' (e.g., 30 days, 100 operating hours), baseline energy_per_ton measurement (before or after NGHE Solution deployment), and whether E_target is a single aggregate value or a time-series threshold.
Tasking interface scope clarification: Confirm whether efficiency-based recommendations (Tasking) are in scope for this FR or deferred to a separate FR. If deferred, remove Tasking from IM boundary.

Computation latency acceptance: Document the acceptable delay between data collection and ratio availability. If real-time efficiency feedback is required, confirm that this is a separate FR and not in scope for the current DP.

Safety criticality mapping: Clarify whether high safety criticality applies to efficiency reporting accuracy, data completeness, or downstream closed-loop control. If the latter, defer to a separate FR and revise safety criticality of current FR to 'medium' or 'low'.
